In captivity the anxiety can get even worse.
So giving them a dog companion, especially something like a Golden Retriever whose main goal in life is to make friends and adopt everything and anything can be really beneficial to a Cheetah.
So yes, these big Cats, the largest breed of the Small Cats family actually, do need a puppy of their own.

video_description: Can you have a pet cheetah? Answering a common question about exotic pets and big cat ownership OMote Cordless leaf blower ...
text_content: What Jon says here confirms what I have heard from other sources.  Setting aside the problem of how to ethically purchase a healthy cheetah cub, the biggest challenge with maintaining a healthy cheetah in captivity is properly sourcing their diet.  If cheetahs do not receive the proper mix of proteins and other nutrients, they will experience various chronic health problems and experience shortened life spans.  From a personality and instinct perspective, cheetahs are the easiest big cat to adapt to a human environment.  Far easier in fact than tigers, lions, leopards, jaguars or pumas, who are potentially very dangerous even when hand-raised from infancy by their humans.  If socialized from cubs to interact with humans, cheetahs will bond and imprint on their human family, and interact like an overgrown house cat.  That said, they instinctually want to mark their territory -- and a 75- to 125-pount cheetah can produce a lot of urine.  They also want to claw and chew inanimate objects.  These are not "indoor" cats; they need to be kept outside a human home, with access to appropriate open space for exercise and play.  At best, having a pet cheetah is an expensive luxury that requires a significant input of cash, time and effort that is beyond the reach of most human beings to provide.

text_content: I recommend gentle dominance with cats.  Most felines don’t comprehend punishment very well and usually will listen out of fear or be anti-social.  If a cat is being persistently hazardous and/ or destructive, I usually pick them up by supporting under the chest with one hand and gently picking them up by the scruff of their neck and relocating.  I try to use physical behavior only when necessary though and calling their name or yelling is usually the first resort.  I’ve met a lot of very aggressive animals in my life who were abused but if you give them space and occasional treats they’ll usually open up to you on their own, just don’t rush it and move slow and in sight if it’s a particularly antisocial animal.  Be calm and relaxed but also prepared to pull away if any defensive aggression is displayed towards you.  Dominance discipline is usually not needed because the right behavior will display dominance.  When you provide food, set boundaries, and occupy your territory, a smaller animal can usually sense you’re the boss unless you act too enabling

video_title: Man Lives With His Two Pet Gibbons
video_description: ID: 3447750 ONSCREEN CREDIT - @eldar.gadjiev This man lives with two pet gibbons in his home in Moscow, Russia. Designer ...
text_content:
There is a gibbon sanctuary not far from my house. Even though gibbons are beyond adorable, fascinating, and even though humans and gibbons are both primates, people CANNOT be replacement gibbons. Gibbons thrive in their natural habitat, NOT as expensive novelty pets. Gibbons kept in captivity often develop behaviors that their owners can't handle when they grow up. Pet baby gibbons are frequently separated from their mothers before they are ready to be independent just for someone's amusement. 
The owner of the gibbon sanctuary by my house told me that you can tell which gibbons were separated from their mothers as infants because they will suck their thumbs. The gibbons at this sanctuary are descendants of generations of captive pet gibbons. The sanctuary is a full time, intense job and labor of love for the lady who keeps the sanctuary. People have absolutely no idea how labor intensive these adorable tiny gibbons will grow up to be. Even the most attuned human will absolutely NOT be a gibbon mother who can teach a gibbon how to be a gibbon. This baby gibbon's vocalizations were tragic, because this baby gibbon is instinctually calling out for other gibbons to socialize with, especially its mother. 
As much as I adore gibbons, keeping this tiny baby gibbon as an amusing pet is beyond narcissistic and short sighted. Gibbons in a house will always need a diaper, because I have never seen or heard if one who could be potty trained.

text_content:
No.  Monkeys - in general - do NOT make "good" pets.

Unless you are very well-prepared and aware of what monkeys are and do, don't get a monkey. They are too intelligent, curious, and possessive to be a good room mate. They will shit and piss anywhere, and at any time.  You cannot physically discipline a monkey, nor is it easy to do so by restricting them from activity or out of cage freedoms. They will bear a grudge. They will bite if you attempt to lift it off the couch, and throw their shit at you as you pass the cage.

This is why they end up mean and vicious, and needing rescue.

DON'T get a monkey.

text_content:
Although the actions depicted in this video are viewed as harmless, animal experts assert that these animals experience high levels of stress from being tortured during the course of such "performances": Dr. Anna Nekaris, an anthropologist who specializes in slow-loris research at Oxford Brookes University states that the loris willingness to be held is not because they are comfortable but rather it is part of their defense mechanism — to be still and silent when they feel threatened. The only reason the loris isn't biting the person holding it in the video is because it has had its teeth cruelly ripped out by illegal traders who poach them from their natural rain forest habitat. The teeth are removed because the loris can deliver a toxic bite. The reason the tickled slow loris is seen raising its arms (a sign of distress) is because it is making an attempt to defend itself by reaching its toxic gland: The slow loris is the world's only poisonous primate. Its venom is stored in an elbow patch. When it is feeling threatened, the slow loris will raise its arms above its head in a diamond shape, suck in the poison from its elbow patch, then mix it around in its mouth before delivering the toxic bite. These animals are also often in pain because they are nocturnal and not equipped to deal with extended periods of daylight. In addition to causing a high level of stress in the animal, this video also encourages the illegal trade of slow lorises: The popularity of slow loris videos has led to an increase in the number of people wanting to have a slow loris as a pet. And while it may appear cute, the animal in the video is suffering and so is the entire species. Slow lorises are shoved into plastic 
crates and bags; their teeth are clipped out with pliers or wire cutters with no anesthesia, no aftercare, no medicine and most die; multiple animals are crammed into tiny cages where they wound each other with their venomous bites. Large percentages of captured slow lorises die due to inhumane, overcrowded, stressful conditions during transportation often with the bodies of others that have died on the journey. This painful process of mutilation not only often kills the animals via blood loss or infection, but also prevents rescued lorises from being released back into the wild. Not only does the slow loris pet trade cause unimaginable suffering, it is also the biggest threat to the survival of the species, which is in serious danger of extinction. All eight species of slow lorises are considered “threatened” and the Javan slow loris is one of the 25 most endangered primates worldwide. So if you have a heart, please do not like or share this video.

text_content:
Slow loris
"Their huge round eyes, adorable faces and almost human-like paws have made slow lorises one of the internet's favourite animals. Being nocturnal, the little primates seem almost docile during the day: when they do move around, they do so slowly and deliberately. When threatened, they tend to freeze.
But slow lorises also have a lethal ace up their sleeve, almost literally.
In extreme cases, bite recipients may enter anaphylactic shock, sometimes resulting in death
When threatened, a loris will often raise its arms above its head. Far from being a sign of surrender, this is a defensive posture, bringing a gland around its elbow close to its mouth.
By combining the oil secreted from the gland with saliva, the loris creates a strong venom, which it then smears onto the top of its head, making it a toxic morsel for any predator.
The loris can also keep the venom inside its mouth, delivering it in a bite. The incisors at the front of the bottom jaw help to conduct the liquid upwards, ensuring it gets into any wound it can inflict.
According to Anne-Isola Nekaris of Oxford Brookes University, a bite from a slow loris is "intensely painful". "In extreme cases, bite recipients may enter anaphylactic shock, sometimes resulting in death," Nekaris says.
There are nine known species of slow loris living in the forests of South East Asia. The venom of the pygmy slow loris (Nycticebus pygmaeus) has been found to contain 220 compounds.
So, the next time you watch a video of a slow loris being "tickled" with its arms in the air, remember it is not enjoying the experience: it is waiting for a chance to strike."

video_title: Do SQUIRREL MONKEYS make good PETS?
video_description: Who hasn't dreamt about having a pet monkey? In this video, we look into what it actually takes to have a squirrel monkey as a pet ...
text_content: Knew a family who had a tiny squirrel monkey for almost 30 years.  He slept in his cage but knew how to open the door so essentially ran free in their house.   He was housetrained to use his cage which was lined with newspaper but preferred the cats' litterboxes (they also had up to 3 cats).  The cats initially tried stalking him because he was tiny but he quickly demonstrated that he could put some serious hurt on them if he wanted to.   He could be destructive, and I wouldn't recommend one as a pet to anyone else.  But they had a large family with a lot of kids around to play with him and keep an eye on him.  They had to keep their cupboards locked because he knew how to open them and would tear open cereal boxes and smash peanut butter and jelly jars on the floor if given the opportunity.  He had astonishing speed, leaping ability, and coordination and so 'won' almost any game of tag or keep-away he played with the kids.  He escaped the house a few times and was un-catchable. text_content:
I'd prefer that people require training/permits to acquire and raise certain common "exotic" pet species.  
Sadly, with the tragic trend of infant macaques in Cambodia and various parts of Indonesia being abducted and sold, these remarkable species are being exploited, tormented, starved, and abused on film by sadists who appeal to those who prefer to watch this abuse in their free time.  
Monkeys are extremely social and they require more care, attention, and affection than most people are willing (and able) to offer...  
As much as I would love to experience the challenge of raising a monkey from infancy, I also know that my family and my job require the most of my attention.  If I couldn't provide the best possible life for an animal, I would not acquire the animal.  
Pets are cohabitating companions, they are not accessories...

text_content: Bless your heart for rescuing all these animals. I did what one should never do. I bought an exotic pet without doing any research first because it was a baby and adorable. That pet was an Umbrella or Great White Cockatoo. Fortunately I was willing to put in the work and learn everything I could about them and how to care for them properly. Parrots of all kinds, but especially the different cockatoos, are a lot of work. You can’t just put them in a cage and feed them seeds. If you do you get a very socially unadjusted and sick bird. After all this research and putting into practice what I had learned with my parrot I began to look around and see all the parrots that were being neglected, abused and abandoned because people didn’t realize how much work they were, how long they lived (they are a lifetime pet) and the novelty of owning one had worn off. text_content: Excellent advice. We've had six Amazons (two napes, three blue fronts, one double yellow head), most of them for decades (we both did rescue work for many years, which is how they acquired us), and while they're quite different than greys, there are a lot of overlaps. With most parrots, it's much better to have at least two so they can keep each other company when you're gone. (Do not put them in a single cage, even temporarily--that can lead to disaster.) Also, they need a lot of time out of their cages--at least four hours a day. (We leave ours out every waking hour except when we're gone.) And they're incredibly destructive, with a seemingly special taste for rare books, LPs, CDs, furniture, kitchen cabinets, baseboards, doors, etc., etc. So, if you have parrots, unless you're the type of jerk who'll keep them locked up, be prepared for a lot of destruction: they're greater teachers regarding the unimportance of physical things. Finally, do not clip their wings. They're happier and more confident when they're flighted; you'll need to be vigilant about keeping doors closed (screen and security doors are a good idea), but they'll be happier for it.

The other thing is the Gray is a very dusty bird that has this very fine dander they use to keep clean with fewer baths.  While not needing so many baths is good it also means it tends to be quite dusty around the birds cage or perch.  Sadly the Amazon while very friendly with me and other men for some reason it did not like women at all and would actively attack them (including my wife) and when we had kids I was just not willing to risk the bird injuring an infant so it had to go.  I truly miss having the parrot now but always think fondly of the time I had with them in my life.

text_content: I'm temporarily staying in Turkey and I saw an African Grey parrot in a cage outside a jewellery store today. Turkey has quire a warm climate, but it's still winter here and people are wearing winter coats... I bought a banana and fed it to the parrot. After eating some of it, he forgot about the rest and all he wanted was affection from me and my husband. We stayed with him for around 15 minutes, scratched him and told him how beautiful he was. He became visibly happier, ruffled his feathers, his pupils started changing size and he even started to dance. I told his owner (who also owns the jewellery store) that he cannot be kept outside during winter because he may get a respiratory disease. She didn't speak any English, but I used a translator. She seemed surprised and grateful for the advice and she brought the cage inside the store. Still, it's so frustrating. You shouldn't get these beautiful and intelligent animals if you don't know how to care for them. They are a huge responsibility - they are like children. The same goes for any other kind of animal. Please, please, don't get one before you educate yourself on how to care for them! They are sentient beings and very much capable of suffering both physically and mentally. They are not toys or just something pretty to look at.

text_content: I have one CAG that I have wanted for years. I got him when he was 13w-14w old (he just turned 1) he is my first parrot. I had done tons of research over about a 2 year time span of learning everything I could. I decided to add one to my family.  There were a lot of people who frowned not only on me getting a baby vs. rescuing but also choosing a grey as my first bird. It is honestly so sad that bigger birds are shown as a "advanced/intermediate" level no bird is an easy pet. Don't expect everything to always be fun and easy, there are always rough patches. All parrots require a lot of time and engagement but are so worth the effort in the end. Every species has both advantages and disadvantages and no one bird is right for everyone. Parrots live a very long time, it doesn't make sense to get one bird just to help prepare you for another ( the one you have your heart set on) and also is not really fair for that bird. Please always do your research. However, don't let talk turn you away from the bird you want regardless of if you are a first time owner or not. I am so happy with my grey and I would have done the same thing looking back. It breaks my heart to think I could have compromised with smaller "beginner bird" and not have my baby Yoshi today.  Ill end this rant here although I could go on...lol Reflecting, I wish I would have had this perspective when I was deciding to get my first bird.

text_content: I just got my 2 parrots as rescues from the parrot trust Scotland called Cody and coco. In 4 weeks Cody has managed to stop taking haldol medication for the first time in years and coco has opened up to men when before she would only interact with women. I’m so proud of the work I’ve done. They where both caged parrots and didn’t want to come out their cages for anything. Not food motivated so was tough. Now when I go in to their bedroom they literally bang their beaks on the cage to get out and come be where I am. text_content:
I have a rescued Moluccan cockatoo, Kiah (Australian Aboriginal for "From the beautiful place."). I've had her since the Summer of '05. She was an import in the 60s, and was used as a breeding hen for the pet trade, and overbred to the point of bone and beak deformaties. When she was no longer of any use as a breeder, they dumped her, and because she was never socialized, she bounced from house to house. When I got her, nobody could go near her, because she would go ballistic. I did what I do with all my rescues; I set her cage up next to my computer, and fed her, watered her, and kept her cage clean, but other than that, I didn't look at her, talk to her, anything. It was about her observing me. Within two months, she made the decision to approach me and make first contact, and since then she's what we who work with toos call a "love sponge." She's a massive cuddler, will open her wings to give a hug (she was not taught this), and loves meeting most people.

That's the positive. She's also loud. For those curious, Moluccans are capable of being louder than the Umbrella Clint showed in the video. Moluccans are capable of screaming at 135dB, which is only 50dB louder than what can cause permanent hearing damage. They also stomp their feet when they are upset (okay, that's kind of adorable), which is almost always followed by screaming. Like many rescues, she's also a plucker/self mutilator, and wears a styling collar to keep the damage limited to only *looking* like she went through a blender, and not giving her the more damaging effects of having actually met the business end of her built-in one, also known as her beak. With the collar, she only does damage to her feathers; without it, she will tear into her skin. 

Basically, while I love my Kiah, and will have her until one of us croaks, I can say this with *total* confidence; you rated them high, Clint.

text_content:
Thank you for this video, especially your emphasis on how much responsibility and dedication these birds require to keep properly.

When I was a kid I was friends with someone whose parents owned a couple of cockatoos. The birds were very neglected and it made me incredibly sad. I never saw them leave their cages, and those cages were in a walk-in-closet sized room covered in bird dust, so they were extremely poorly socialized. The larger of the cockatoos would let me stroke him through his cage if I was careful, the smaller would hiss and attempt to bite if I came anywhere near her enclosure. Apparently the owners initially took better care of them, my friend told me when she was young the birds were frequently let out of their enclosures and would even accompany the owners on car rides around town (not the safest thing but at least the birds were getting attention and activity). I suspect the owners got tired of attending to the birds' needs after the novelty wore off or after the owners had children.

Animals are a commitment, not a decoration or a toy to be tossed aside when they get boring or when life gets stressful; people need to know what they're getting into and make sure they're capable of providing for an animal for its entire life, *especially* for animals that are high-needs. I am glad to see some other commenters here have changed their minds about getting a pet cockatoo, and I hope anyone who is still interested in owning one will understand how much time and work they'll need to put in to take proper care of these amazing birds.

I still remember the time he bit through the iron bars of his cage and escaped into the backyard. text_content:
Great info! Pretty much completely accurate. I would add a few things (as someone who's worked at a specialty bird shop for 7+ years)-

1. You mentioned cockatoos are very dusty. If anyone in the household has asthma, the cockatoo will most likely trigger allergies. Even people who aren't allergic can actually develop allergies to their birds. We've been trying to rehome a goffins cockatoo whose parents LOVED her, but her mom developed lung issues and cannot be in close proximity to birds anymore. Three other cockatoos this year have been surrendered just to our shop for similar health issues (and more were surrendered for other reasons) 

2. Cuddling a cockatoo all the time can cause horrible behavioral issues. Be careful how you cuddle a cockatoo, as petting them in certain areas or giving them too many kisses can make them hormonal. Not only is it frustrating and confusing for the bird, they will lash out and bite.

3. Having multiple birds to keep each other company is not as simple as buying multiple birds. They will have to start in separate cages, be supervised when together, and you will have to make sure it is safe before letting them live in the same cage together.

4. Cockatoos can be extremely jealous. If they choose you as their favorite person, they may be aggressive to your loved ones or anyone they perceive to be an obstacle to your affection. Unfortunately, sometimes you will have to choose between your loved ones or your bird.

text_content: I have 6 of them, all adopted. There is a reason why people get rid of them. Their snuggling is because they are not socialized the natural way, and they demand this every day. Every new cage lasts a few weeks and then it needs new doors , latches or gates.They can pop locks and cage-bars and destroy your woodwork in minutes.

It sticks out to me after watching certain cockatoo channels for a while, when one of them talked about how he acquired his Cockatoo (fans will immediately recognise this as being Max) and his owner admitted how it wasn't planned, he was just caring for him for a friend, but it turned out now working out as his friends bird and the bird became attached to him. They never get older and more independent, and they require the same amount of care and attention every day.

They're great birds for cute videos, but they must be exhausting pets.  Even for the experienced, for this reason. 

You can't just get tired and stick them in a cage to get away from them and some piece for a bit, and they're smart enough to resent you if you do.

Beautiful birds, but only the most equipped and capable should care for them. Every animal deserves a fulfilling life, but it seems the cockatoo has particularly higher needs for enrichment and stimulation than most other pets. Why it's likened to having to constantly care for a toddler. 

Mad respect for those who give these birds the life they deserve

I got my due to last owner couldn’t tame her and she sent one of them to the hospital. I ended up taming her, but ended up buying the bird from them. I dealt and train 100’s of parrots. One of the reason cockatoos are my favorite is the fact they are temperamental.

text_content: You know, this is great for me because once upon a long time ago, i worked at a dingy reptile shop that had a few birds. Two african greys, one scarlet macaw, and two umbrella cockatoos. One of which was named Tenguin. And tenguin, like all of the other birds there, was very neglected. He spent 97% of his time inside his dirty, empty, bird cage. He maybe had one toy in there at a time. Consequently, he was a very energetically frustrated bird that would constantly scream for attention because human interaction was the only time he got to do anything with all that energy. Peekaboo, singing songs, trading phrases, playing tag, getting treats, etc. Poor little guy all around. They all were, but i he took it the hardest. And his scream. Good god, his scream was so loud that it could be heard from the inside of the co-op, across a parking lot, and through the shut windows and doors of our building. It was so frequent, all the co op staff knew his name and could accurately identify him from inside their building solely by how loud his caw screetch was. I was 18 years old when i worked there. I spent a lot of time with tenguin trying to help entertain him because i didn't know what else i could do, he wasn't my bird and i knew nothing about birds. He ended up bonding with me during my time there. Well, the shop wasn't in a great financial situation in general, they had too many reptiles. So when covid hit they went under. I go back to visit a little before that finalizes, I'm 20ish right about then and next thing i know is I'm being sales pitch'd to buy Tenguin. 3k and I can have him and his gross ass cage! What a deal. Nevermind the fact that I was living in a rental room, had no space or knowlege about how to realistically care for birds. I almost considered buying him just to have the opportunity to give him to an avian rescue i knew of through my own exotics contacts. But 3k i didn't have to rehome a bird? That was the cruddy part. It sucks because i did genuinely love that bird, and in waaay different circumstances i might have took him on and figured it out as i went just to get him out of there, but honestly, just no. I don't know where he is now, and that sucks. I hope he finally found his way into better hands. But I dodged a BULLET.

text_content:
Cockatoo are flock birds, very social, very active, very destructive and very loud. If you leave one by her/his self, they will squawk insistently at 135 decibels and can be heard up to 5 miles away in ideal conditions. In the wild, they communicate with squawks, happy cockatoos squawk. Don't be fooled by people claiming only unhappy cockatoos squawk. 
I see wild flocks daily, and grew up in a household with a well cared for pet cockatoo.
Cockatoos are happiest in a flock. A human or two with a cage does not replace a flock and freedom.

text_content: When I had a pet Quaker, he made friends with several wild birds in the neighbourhood.  Every day at around 2 PM, a beautiful Blue Jay would land on my balcony, perch on the rail and get as close to the balcony door as possible.  He would hang out and chat back and forth with Beetle and it was adorable to see.  Every day, always within 10 minutes of 2 PM.  After we lost Beetle in an accident, the Blue Jay continued to come to the balcony looking for him for nearly a month.  It was heartbreaking - the Jay would desperately try to get into my apartment, almost like he thought I was keeping Beetle from him.  Seeing this video reminded me f their bond and just how amazing it was.

text_content: I am American and we adopted a cockatoo from a pet shop when she was 3 months old.  She hopped on my daddy's shoulder. So of course he had to bring her home!  We had her for 15 years.  My father - her primary owner got dementia and fell with her 2 times on his shoulder.  She got quite upset since she really loved my dad.  My mother and I also loved her and held her and played with her a lot.  She was only in her cage at bed time till morning.  My parents are in their 80's and I am 63 with a serious lung disease and stomach disease. It was getting hard to give her the quality of life care we believed she should have.  A wonderful young woman comes in twice month to clean for us for about 2 years.  Our bird took to her.  We gave her the bird, cage, all of our bird books, food and my mother made a notebook - how we fed her, human snacks (a couple times a week I would give her a very small piece of my pecan sandy cookie!), no-no foods, vet info, games we played, words she knows and the songs we sang to her and the one song she has learned 3 words to!  And the shop we got her from will trim her nails and wings for free. She is doing well.  It has been about 2 years and my daddy barely remembers her.  He named her after his well-loved grandmother Judy.  My momma and I still exchange memories of Judy.  And her 'new' owner shows us videos and tells about the new words she has learned and everything else wonderful she does!

text_content: My mom had a Malaccan cockatoo when I was a teen. He basically become mine, I'm the only one who would feed him, take care of him, play with him. My mom kicked me out at 16 for two years. I cam back and she told me stay out of the "bird room". I had to sneak in there and I found him almost starved to death, with his feathers ripped out. I convinced her to give him to a sanctuary while I secretly nursed him back to health. I still cry sometimes thinking about what it must have been like to live locked up in a cage for two years, starving. I wish people would stop buying birds if they're not going to give them all the attention they need.

text_content:
Many thanks to the caring couple who welcomed this feathered beauty into their lives!  Edgar will return all that love tenfold; you can tell he's a real sweetheart.  

When I saw the cage he was kept in for so long, my heart just broke for him; I cannot ever imagine keeping a bird in cage that small.  All parrots are incredibly intelligent, & cockatoos especially are mechanically inclined, so they require a great deal of mental stimulation (think unlock locks, unscrewing your doorknobs, & bolts & nuts; they love that kind of thing).  I'm so glad Edgar is with people who will love him, & give him the best life possible.  He may not be able to fly, but the love this family will share will make their hearts soar!

text_content:
1.  They're loud.  Some owners use gun range ear sets to cancel out the noise.  It's not enough to lighten the sound when the bird screams on days when you have a headache, want to get away from it.  The sound is meant to travel for over 10 miles in the wild.   Very loud.  You have neighbors?   Live in an apartment?  Your landlord and neighbors will hate you.  There will be days YOU will hate the noise too.
2.  Expensive.   A bird this size needs a flight cage.   A cage no smaller than a king size bed.  You have room for that?   They also need regular toe and beak grooming.  If you're not capable of doing it, can you afford to take the bird to a vet and get it done once a month at least?  Beak and toes also need coconut oil massages, regularly.  You feel like doing that for 80 years?   They live up to 80 years.   Who takes care of the bird if you die suddenly?   Got plans for that?  And they need a good diet.   Chewing is a must to keep beak in good condition.  They MUST chew.  So supplying them with toys or hard nuts is not optional, it's a must-have.  Can you afford their diet?  Or are you going to be a bad owner, that gives them the bare minimum for them to survive?   Too lazy to do fresh fruit and vegetables for them daily?  Don't get a bird.  And don't think you can skip the cage.  They're a 2 year old that never grows up.   Ever.  They need a time out cage.  It's their chill out zone.
3.  Time consuming.  You MUST read at least a pile of bird training books and behavior books BEFORE adopting a bird so you know the signs.  Training the bird takes time.  And has to be done a few minutes every day.   This bird depends on you.   You need to KNOW what you will do to this bird if you abandon it, go off on vacation, leave the bird behind, what medical problems you will be inflicting on your bird.  Plucking is only the most common you'll read about.  You need to read a pile of books BEFORE YOU ADOPT A BIRD so you can also be wise to medical problems.  You need to recognize medical issues long before they're obvious.   A bird can go south quickly.   These are not birds you can abandon, no large bird is.  And you're shit if you adopt one and abandon it.

4.  Bored bird = living terror!   Are you smarter than a 5th grader?  If not, don't get a bird.  Need to be smarter than the bird in order to keep it mentally stimulated and out of trouble.   Need to be emotionally mature enough to be able to handle a 2 year old tossing a tantrum without loosing your cool.  Got a temper?  Don't get a bird.
5.  KNOW the people you're getting the bird from.   Are they good people?   Are you accidentally supporting bird breeders that are flooding the market for a profit, not caring what happens to the babies?  It's the same thing as a puppymill.   Are you backing that?  Now days you have to be careful of even adoption agencies for these birds.   Even some of them are corrupt.   Do you know?  Done your homework?  I won't do it for you.   That,  you research yourself.    In order for it to stop, people need to get wise to it.  And they won't get wise unless they do the leg work.

text_content: I have one and i regret getting one everyday, my family decided to get African gray so we went to a pet shop and bought a new born African gray, the bird turned out to be disabled and it had heart problems so the shop gave us our money back. They offered us a baby Umbrella cockatoo instead and we took it. The cockatoo was a female and she was so big compared to regular cockatoos. We didn’t know how bad cockatoos were before we bought them we just had conures before. My pet cockatoo is 7 now and she just has so many issues. She gets super sexual with us so we cant touch her below the neck, we learned that the hard way after she laid 4 eggs between the ages of 4-6💀 terrible ik. We trained her to stay on her cage with our other bird which is a nanday conure. They get alone just fine and rarely fight but when they do she always does something horrible like once she bite a hole in the other birds beak. She has never not once bit a human before though. She so loving and sweet but also a demon at the same time. She has gotten out and ruined tv remotes and cords before. She also has butt problems because she prolapse herself whenever she feels like she doesn’t have enough attention. We had to result into spraying her with water because she pushes her organ out if she doesnt get sprayed. She has been doing her butt thing for years now and she never cleans her butt because she started at a young age. Not her fault at all by the way she has behavior issues because we gave her too much attention. I was like 10 when we got her and I’m 17 now. Lover her to death but she would be better off in the wild 😂. She’s not aggressive at all and she is well trained, if we say go up she runs up her cage, if we say get in the cage she runs right in.  We fixed her sexual issue by not letting her on the couch with us. She has to stay on the cage, once in a whole we’ll let her sit on our knee on the couch but other then that she stays on her cage. She chose my father from day one because he never feared her at all and he is also the person she fears the most. She loves my dad but also fears my dad so much, we don’t abuse her at all btw she just fears my dad because he has a big beard and a deep voice and he’s very tall. She has eacaped the cage so many times but recently she has been escaping her cage and staying on top of her cage with the other bird which is progress 😂. She’s just starting to become better because we try to not cuddle her or anything and she talks sooooo much. She got her butt problem original from a stomach illness btw, it started from that and we gave her attention when she would push her stuff out, now she thinks that’s like the bell that get’s everyone’s attention. She is a handful and i regret wanting her lol. (Edit) btw i just wanted to mention she has a huge cage with a nice special structure on top where she chills for most of the day and it’s in the living room so she sees us all day and she gets attention all the time.

text_content: I've had a cockatoo and I've had a blue front Amazon.  I would never let them be close to each other. Cockatoos are extremely intelligent birds. They have the emotional intelligence of a 5-year-old. Their  jealousy drives them to harm or kill their rival which could be a guinea pig or a hamster, or a cat or a dog.  They will go after them if they think that you're not giving them enough attention and love. I ended up giving our cockatoo to a vet lab technician who had always wanted a cockatoo and she had no other animals so it's a perfect home for him.  So this cockatoo is not showing dominance only, he is showing that he will attack and he will harm that bird.  The cockatoo I had, named Kazoo,  had to be pulled off of my African Grey, named Pepper, and he had punctured her cheek and her wing.  And that was not the first incident I had with a cockatoo he went after my darling little Harley bird who is what we call a pocket parrot.  He hurt Harley so badly in his wing we had to amputate the wing as it was destroyed. Harley was a feisty little parrot and he climbed off of his cage one day and marched over to Kazoos cage because he was going to finish what Kazoo had started and I'm so glad I was there to scoop him up and lock him back in his cage or he would have been killed that day.  Something I find very appalling on the internet with animals is people get animals they don't understand they don't know anything about their breed or what the needs are for that individual animal.  I handled Birds since I was 10 years old and I'm in my 70s now and I am appalled at how little people understand are know about birds they are highly intelligent more so than a dog is believe it or not.

They're less like dogs or cats, and more like having a flying, screaming toddler with bolt cutters attached to their faces.

video_title: DON’T buy a Macaw!!! Unless... || Mikey The Macaw
video_description: Don't buy a macaw!! Unless”, is a video we felt we had to make which shows some of the real things about owning a macaw or ...
text_content:
As a owner of a parrot for 12 years:
1.) Macaws are the LOUDEST parrots in the rainforest. So they will scream EXTREMELY LOUD when they play, want attention, are upset, when they want your food, ect.
2.) Their intelligence is the equivalent to a human toddler. They need as much attention, love, & play as a human toddler. If you don't give them this time they will have significant mental health & physical healthy problems.
3.) They go through puberty just like humans. Once the reach puberty (in their early teens) their personality changes. They will become more aggressive, jealous if u hangout with anyone else, and randomly biting. 
4.) They poop on EVERYTHING constantly. They poop every 15 minutes so you have to be ok with them pooping on ur furniture, nice house items, & with deep cleaning the cage every couple of days.
5.) Vet bills are EXTREMELY EXPENSIVE. The average vet bill for me was $400. When she became sick it was around $1,000 each vet visit for mediations & tests.
6.) Their average lifespan in captivity is the human life span. If something happens to you or if u adopt a young macaw they will out live you. They have SEVERE PTSD if they have to switch owners because of the deep emotional connection. This can lead to self mutilation.
7.) They love only 1 human at a time. They will be jealous & hate any other humans you are close to.

as a birdowner for more than twenty years I can confirm, that everything told in the video is absolutely right!
 I just want to add, if you get a parrot,

No one can groom feathers so wonderful as another bird can!

In my country (germany) it is even forbidden to keep parrots individually.

To expect something like this from your bird is not love, it is just "wanting to have", and these magical creatures do not deserve such a sad life!

Mikeys life has been wonderful so far, but I'm sure his beautiful Mia is the icing on the cake of his life! 🐦🐦💞

text_content: I'm gonna be honest, I agree with the no clipping. My mother has raised me to believe that owning birds that can fly is cruel, unless you have a space where they can fly. They were made to fly so why prevent them from doing so? If you don't want your pet to fly then why the hell would you get a flying pet? And I agree with the window thing. People say they clip their bird's wings so they won't fly out the window. Why the hell is their window open? It's all so stupid to me to be honest.I do admit that I have had to clip wings. But it was the wings of a small chicken. I never expected her to fly like she did and we have neighbors with dogs. It broke my heart to have to clip her wings but chickens weren't exactly made for flying in the first place. (And we didn't know how flighty her breed was when we got her.)In the end, if you don't have a place for your bird to fly and it's not safe, don't get a flying bird. Just don't do it! Let someone who can take care of a flying bird do it! It's that simple. If you love them, you'll let them fly. If it's not safe where you are, be caring enough to let them fly elsewhere.

text_content: Wonderful video on how to be a great friend to these awesome birds. I am a board member for Exotic Bird Rescue of Oregon (USA) and am blessed with a loving Catalina Macaw myself. People, please listen very closely to this message. It is wonderfully put. On average, exotic birds are re-homed, more than 5 times before finally finding a 'forever' home. and the effects on them can be horrendous. Everything from self-mutilation (feather plucking and even tearing holes in their own flesh) to nearly un-controllable screaming and aggression. (Nearly...  a lot of work and love can remedy a lot of problems).  Also, find a bird club in your area and get some experience with them. Learning to read body language can save the bird and YOU a lot of blood and tears.  I only have one... small... disagreement, and that is with clipping wings and free flighting. I agree that clipping wings seem a travesty but there are situations where, for the safety of the bird, I would recommend it. Also being part of a rescue I can assure you that even well-trained birds get scared and fly away on occasion... sometimes never to be seen (alive at least) again. We get several calls a week regarding birds that 'have never flown off before' having been scared and are gone....it is heartbreaking. If you do take your bird outside....please PLEASE think about an appropriate flight harness.

People should learn about the needs of their future companion and also get exposed to the noise and judge if they can handle it. You'd think that I got wise after it happened once or twice, but it happened over and over. The reason is simple. There was a pet in need that the owner wanted to get rid of and I felt I had the space, the time and the level of commitment to take it in. 
I'm glad it never was a parrot because I'm not sure I could have handled that without being the one being unhappy in the relationship. Because once I commit I stick with it especially if the pet bonded with me and relied on me to take proper care. I like birds and I enjoyed living with a "flock" of 6 budgies that spent most of the day on a bird tree in the living room outside their cage. But that's worlds apart from living with a parrot. So I'm gonna stick watching parrots on YouTube enjoy other people loving their birds.

text_content:
Yep! I actually got my girl free, because the person who had her was having problems with her causing all of her other birds to get into trouble and then sitting back and chilling. Billy had the run of my house, and was actually pretty quiet. And at my house, you'd see the bird chasing the cats, not the cats chasing the bird. BUT....I will say that the small cage has it's use in an emergency. When i had been up north for a bit, and took Billy with me, we had a power outage and ice storm. The house dropped to 20 degrees f at night. We kept her warm at night by putting her in the the small emergency cage in front of the fireplace, put plastic gallons of hot water around it then threw quilts over it. Kept her warm all night, which I couldn't have done otherwise. 
Billy wasn't that destructive either. Even when no one was home (and she still had full run of the house). But she had the cats and a dog to play with and I always left a radio on or TV, so there was always someone around for her. And I agree about the wings. Don't clip.. she came with clipped wings and when she'd try to fly, she'd end up hitting a wall or door. Letting her have her wings back ended that issue. And, towards the end of her life, she became "mommy" to 3 orphaned kittens and an orphaned newborn goat (it was dead winter and I had her in the house so she'd survive). She'd preen them, discipline them, curl up and sleep with them...Basically did everything a mom would do but feed them and was their "mom" for the rest of her life. She ended up dying from a series of strokes. As she was already an adult when I got her, and she was an adult when the other person had gotten her, I had no clue as to her age. She was burried with my dog and potbellied pig, who were her 2 best buddies for a good part of her life!

text_content:
One correction: Macaws live around 30 years in the wild, but can live up to 100 years in captivity. They are wild animals and as such are very difficult pets to care for, and only an experienced exotic bird owner should own one.

They’re incredibly intelligent, friendly, and loyal, but require as much attention and work as a small child, AND they can literally deafen you with their incredibly loud screams, _AND_ are capable of biting through a broomstick with their beaks.

I don’t say this to be mean to macaws, but because I rescue parrots, and strongly believe you ought to require a license to own certain larger species which are sold through the exotic pet trade. Macaws and cockatoos often end up having at least two, three, even four plus owners in their lifetimes because people think it will be like owning a dog, but can’t handle the noise or the biting or the constant care needed, and the poor macaws end up homeless in a pet rescue facility.

*Please do NOT buy a macaw. If you are committed to owning one, ALWAYS rescue a homeless bird instead of buying one!*

text_content:
I grew up in a macaw household. They can live to 100 in captivity, and often outlive their owners. My parents had 3 when I was born, and they re-homed 2 of them because 4 children was too much for them. As a result, I had a scarlet macaw named Kimba in my home until she passed from a stroke when I was about 4. I still remember my mom in tears, holding the colorfulbird in her arms, unable to save her. Not long after, we purchased a B&G macaw from a breeder. I remember walking into the "pet store" in Hollywood, and seeing several pin-feathered birds on a table. That was 1983. The pin-feathered creature we took home that day became my bird brother, and still lives with my dad. He has arteriosclerosis, and must be medicated to prevent/delay a fatal stroke.

They are incredible animals, with the mentality of a human 2y/o for their entire lives. They can be very loving and cuddly, protective and loyal, and super destructive, all in the same day. If you're thinking of adopting a macaw, treat the decision with the same seriousness as the thought of adopting a special-needs human child. It takes that much dedication, and someone else will need to be ready to take on that responsibility when you die.

text_content:
My RB Cockatoo has arthritis and loves her feet massaged. I'll put coconut oil on her feet everyday, and she's learned to "ask" for the oil by lifting up one of her feet when I'm giving her loves (aka scritches on her head).

Speaking of a bird's head, when they are molting, the shafts get pretty sharp. The bird's head is more sensitive than normal during this time. It's a good idea to gently break the shafts apart by rubbing each of them between the thumb and forefinger. Otherwise, the feathers poke them while you're petting them. This is painful and that can result in a bite, too.

video_description: Whenever my birds & I meet new people, one of their first questions or fears will be about bites. The truth is, they don't want to ...
text_content: I work behavioral issue macaws to help them become adoption candidates. This is very true. Most bites I’ve gotten have come from fear. I’ve worked with macaws who have been through insane amounts of abuse, I’ve worked with birds that would bite everyone and everything, yet they never hurt me to the point of needing stitches or permanent disfigurement. I’ve gotten some nasty bites here and there, I’ve collected a few scars even, but it’s a far cry from the real damage these guys can do. They are pretty gentle by nature. I love seeing how they transform and learn better boundaries as well. I learn  how to keep that bird from feeling the need to bite. Birds will bite but most of the time they give very clear warnings before hand. It definitely is about knowing your bird as well, birds can bite not just because they’re scared but also because they are over stimulated. Be gentle, patient, and kind with a biting bird. Most will learn boundaries over time or at least you will learn how to pick up on the warning signs.

text_content:
I have a very large beautiful male yellow nape  named Cricket.  I have had him for 27 years (he is 28) and I got him from a sanctuary where he'd been placed after being abused.  I think HR is the perfect parrot, but it took a lot of time and work to gain his trust, and he still barely tolerates men.  

He is relatively quiet, and sing and talks and doesn't scream except rarely and only if he senses danger like a hawk or helicopter or cat.and is incredibly loving in his way and extremely affectionate.  I am disabled an have several chronic illnesses including brittle diabetes, and he learned very early to alert me to low blood sugar emergencies by rubbing vigorously on my cheek and saying "it's OK!"  "You alright?" and not stopping until I ate or drank (of course he got jackpot treats!). If it was late or bedtime he would often not go  in his cage, but run to the bedroom and station himself on the headboard of the bed and would want to watch me all night. Getting him in his cage was hard on those nights!   I live in NYC and he is a fixture in the neighborhood,  If he isn't seen for a  few days during good weather,  people come and ask about him.  He constantly flirts with the neighborhood women calling them to. "come see the birdie", and telling them that they're beautiful. His only flaw is that he's messy,but I wouldn't change a thing about him.  He is a true blessing in my life,  and he also brings smiles to almost everyone who sees him!

We have been together since and its great, he is well known in the neighborhood as we take walks morning and evening when the temperature allows it (Canada). His name is Picchu or ''Sir what are you doing'' when he is causing mayhem. He is around 5 years old (i dont have paper on him)       

My cat is still not super ok with him yet but we are working on it.

text_content: I am my Amazons third and last owner.  I got him when he was 7, he is 36 now.  He loves me and hates all others.  He is not a good talker and can be pretty grumpy, and lucky for me, he is on the quiet side.  I was able to live in a lot of apartments with him with no complaints from neighbors….but that isn’t the norm.  My wish is that all people thinking of getting a parrot do their research and make sure they know what they are getting into:  all the healthy food prep, the mess, the noise, the destruction, exotic vet bills, and all the attention they need to keep them happy and well adjusted.  It’s a major commitment, and obviously a lot of people buying parrots do not consider it, or we wouldn’t see such a heartbreaking number of parrots in need of new homes.  Please PLEASE remember these are intelligent, loving creatures who bond very strongly with their owners, it’s not fair to them to not be given the care they deserve, or to be bounced from person to person.

If you think you want a parrot be aware that they live a very long time and also need many hours of attention each day. Do your research and give a new home to a bird from a rescue centre. Do not get a bird if you are out at work all day, they do not deserve to be shut in a cage on their own. Give them love and understanding and they will return it, but by their very nature they will be getting up to mischief and needing constant attention.
A cage should not be a prison but a sanctuary that your bird can use to sleep and feed in.

video_title: Why Parrots May Bond to One Person (And What You Can Do About It)
video_description: A viewer recently asked me about their Orange Wing Amazon parrot who only allows one person (the owner) to pet or touch him.
text_content: So what happens if you never touched your bird inappropriately? I brought home a 7 year old, male Quaker Parrot a year ago. From day one he feigned regurgitation for my fingers and tried to mate with my hands.  I hadn't ever scratched him yet on the head let alone anywhere else. I had researched parrots before getting one so I knew not to rub under the wing and such.  I had only ever had him on my shoulder and perched on my finger. I met him once before to see how he liked me. I spent 3 hours with him before coming back a week later to pick him up.  The first time i met him he flew onto my shoulder, stared me down, gave a head bop and proceeded to rub his beak and face into my beard. I thought he was just friendly but now I suspect he was courting me.  Since bringing him home he frequently gets "excited", even if I just talk to him and even just a short amount of head scratches. I had tried various diets, extra sleep. Attempt to discourage the behavior. I never let him mate with my hand and I will pull away, set him down or put him in the cage but nothing has ever worked.

video_description: The Red Headed Amazon, aka Green Cheeked Amazon, is an insanely fun big personality parrot but the question is, is this the ...
text_content:
What a lovely, insightful video :)

My grandmother got an Amazon back in the eighties and as you might imagine, back then, no one knew how to take care of such a bird. What little information there was, mostly was aimed to make the owners life easier, not ensure the parrot is kept properly. So, after being caught in the wild as a baby, shipped around the world, he spent his first decade in a way too tiny cage, mostly being ignored, because he wasn't handleable and became more and more aggressive over time.
As soon as I was old enough to realize, I tried and succeeded to bond with him. But being a kid still, I too didn't fully understand how to make his life better. Over time, I learned and tried my best to improve his situation, while he still lived with my granny. One day I finally managed to take him with me.
He now has a huge enclosure and since I work from home, he is with me most of the time. I am glad to be able to keep him near me all the time, since finding a bird friend for him was deemed impossible by several experts - he is absolutely terrified of other birds, probably due to what happened during the transport. Alas, he is enjoying attention and love as much as appropriate food. While I try my very best to make his birdy life an awesome one, we still have to work through a lot of issues due to all the years of neglect. But he is doing a great job and is a wonderful friend and I see that he is happy now.
All that being said, I still feel guilty for not helping him sooner, but I will continue to make it up to him.

Seeing you and your peers doing your best to educate people about those animals is just wonderful and I am sure you are saving many of them from ending up in awful situations. Thank you!

video_title: Amazon parrot gets a beak makeover #petbirds #birds #beaktrim
text_content:
I was once given an older Amazon with a similar beak problem; only worse!  He, too, had been raised on a diet primarily of seed, and his beak was so severely overgrown that my Vet said he couldn't trim it back, because he was afraid his nerves may have been involved. 
So, I provided him with a cuddle bone, rock, stick, and put sandpaper on 1 of the perches in his cage, and also attempted to gradually change his diet to a healthier one (as recommended by my Vet), but he didn't use them, and refused to eat. It was sad. He was a miserable, unhappy boy. Soooooo unlike most parrots that we're used to seeing. 

This video was very informative, and I believe he may have died from liver failure. 

I think a lot of people just sincerely don't know that the bird seed that is sold in Pet Stores would be harmful to their birds.

text_content:
Hey there Blue Plant Pets! I just wanted to ask for some help with my bird, since you know a lot about many of them.
I’ve had a few parakeets over the past four years- three total, but sadly two of them died, the last almost a year ago. The parakeet that I have right now is a female yellow-headed, turquoise parakeet, but that isn’t the main problem.
I first got Ari (the parakeet I have right now) along with one other, older parakeet when I was very young. I wasn’t properly trained for these birds, but I at least knew how to feed and water them, and clean their cage. The only thing I wasn’t taught was how to actually take care of them.
The older parakeet had another owner, so she was used to humans. But she died before she could make an impact on Ari. I didn’t properly get her used to me. And although I’ve learned a lot more about taking care of them over the next few years, all the videos or questions or searches have been about first getting birds, not having them for a while.
So, this is the problem I hope you could help me fix. Ari does not let me touch her, let me hold her, or perch on my finger. She most she’ll let me do is hold my finger out for her to gently touch her beak.
Ari has been my friend for years, and I just want to make sure that eventually I’ll be able to show her that I won’t hurt her.

Please and thank you. ❤❤❤

P.S., I am probably going to get a new bird in hopes that if I train the new bird into actually liking me, maybe Ari can learn to like me as well. I don’t know if I will be able to, though.

text_content: I have a rescue red sided eclectus that spent a couple of years (before me) locked in a cage in a closet. I've had Pepper about 2 years now, and he's fully feathered again, stopped his toe tapping and is finally relaxed and happy. After I got him, it took him 2-3 weeks to stop biting me just for the hell of it. It took him about 18 months to actually enjoy being touched and cuddled. He's learned to speak a few more things than when he first arrived, but talking is definitely something that is on his terms. A lot of people don't understand that these birds do EVERYTHING on their own terms and at their own pace. Rescuing an eclectus (or any parrot) is one of the most rewarding things, but OMG does it require patience and persistence. My son rescued a 35 year old triton cockatoo. And i absolutely agree with you here. None of these are beginner birds or for the impatient, but they are amazing birds. But really, nothing beats the morning singing of a sweet little cockatiel. I will always have major love for those guys. I would recommend them every time.

text_content:
Those are depressingly tiny cages. We really need to stop keeping parrots in cages barely wide enough to stretch their wings or walk more than a few steps. Especially something like macaws need to FLY. Even the reptile world is miles ahead of the bird world in giving their animals enriching, naturalistic enclosures with lots of room.
And I don't know why it is, but in 15+ years, I have never seen a pet palm cockatoo that's not a sickly pale pink. In zoos and in the wild, they have blood red cheeks. There must be something wrong with their diets as pets, and I would be very interested to see the lifespan of pet palms.

(Nitpicky, but hawkheads aren't Amazons in any way. They're from South America, but so are macaws and conures. Hawkheads' closest relatives are caiques.)

text_content:
I "rescued" my RES named Dip from a severely neglected pond back when i was 12 years old and she was the size of a silver dollar (I totally didnt know it was bad to take animals out of the wild at the time, but i did know she was invasive and was probably going to die if left in that disgrace of a pond). She's been my roommate ever since and is creeping up to 18 years old soon!

Easily one of my most sociable pets, and demands head/shell scritches when i walk past her. Dip will cause as much noise as possible with her shell and claws on the decor and side of the tank until I pet her, only after sufficient shell scritches and head rubs will she calm down.

They sure are a lot of maintenance compared to other reptiles (so.many.water changes.), but she has definitely been worth it

I know that God brought that turtle to me because of what happened the other day. God’s creation is so beautiful & amazing & most people don’t even see or acknowledge it. Every day as I drive I am in awe of God’s creation all around us!

video_description: My Box Turtle Franklin is the oldest pet I have! At 22 years old I've been caring for him the majority of my life and have learned a ...
text_content: I have a Desert Box Turtle named Diego. She showed up to my back porch full grown with dog chew holes on her shell about 25 years ago and we've been best friends ever since. She's my childhood pet and I still have her living in my room for winter with all her lights in a 75 Gallon Tank. I move her tank to an outside enclosed porch around April because she hates refrigerated air and bring her back inside the first week of October. I never let her play outside alone unless I'm with her because I don't want to lose her. She's about the size of a McDonalds Cheeseburger. I have to admit that when I first got her I had no idea what I was doing, or if she had a family that she never returned to and I feel bad about that but now I'm commited to her because we moved over a decade ago and she came with. I have no idea how she got into the yard because the yard was completely fenced off and there were no holes under the fence. She just appeared at the back door. Now that she's getting older she's very active and happy and healthy but I can't get her to eat anything other than bananas (with bird vitamins 2x a week) and hamburger meat. She loves those but will absolutely not accept any other food. I feed her every day even if she doesn't eat but it's offered. I take her for a yearly check-up every beginning of October at the veterinarian and the doctor says she looks healthy. We're not sure how old she is but she was full grown when she joined my family. I'm Autistic and don't have any children or a wife of my own and I wonder if my childhood beloved pet is going to outlive me. What's she going to do if I die before her? I feel that releasing her into the wild (especially after decades in captivity and in a totally different location) is unthinkable after being in captivity for so long and having her needs taken care of but what's she going to do if I die? I wonder if there's any rescues in New Mexico. Hopefully I don't die anytime soon but she just very well may outlive me. That's a problem.

text_content: I have moved multiple box turtles off the road in the direction they were facing- or if that direction looks dangerous, into nearby woods. Recently I stopped for a 6" male that had a 1" piece of shell broken off and hanging. The wound was dirty and festering a little. He was on a super steep dangerous part of Hwy 52 so I brought him home, cleaned and treated the wound, and glued the shell shard back in place with cyanoacrylate. That held nicely, and I am monitoring him to ensure infection doesnt grow. I made him a 6' outdoor pen with food, water, shade, shelter, and plants. He sure sleeps a lot, and I have not observed him eating, so maybe he is sad, or in pain, or both. Soon as the wound heals fully I will release him (back near where I found him if I can find a safe spot). Thanks for an excellent video!

text_content: Feeding goldfish and Rosie's will eventually kill your turtles. They have a chemical called thiaminase which will build up in the turtle's system and when it gets bad it will display as neurological issues with the turtles. It blocks vitamin B12, and a heavy dose of B12 can fix it if noticed soon enough. My advice is to Google fish that don't contain thiaminase and use those, you can even buy frozen fish, thaw them out and chop them up and the turtles won't see any difference. Also I love how you did an elaborate setup for very common turtles, you're putting people to shame! Many species need different types of cages or temperatures so please keep that in mind. My side job is reptiles in general, monitor lizards and snakes (dozens of species) if you ever need more specific info hit me up. Check out Fox Reptiles if you are ever in Ohio and need a cool video with an insanely rare pretty ass monitor lizard or something. --Steve

text_content: I found a painted turtle on the golf course in spring 2021. It was in the middle of the fairway, bone dry, and the size of a bottle cap, so I put him by the nearest water, over 200 yards away. Came back 20 minutes later and he was still there, in it's shell. I took it home as my 55 gallon tank was almost done cycling, and now he's healthy and well, although missing two toes when I found it, the turtle is a great swimmer. I finally ordered a mercury vapor bulb, as I can't let him bask naturally in the winter for the cold, and lack of sunlight in Michigan. I can't believe my turtle is still alive and I hope it's got years left in the tank!

text_content: I currently have 13 baby painted turtles.  They randomly came up onto my porch, and I collected them.  I do not live that close to water, so I do not know where they came from. text_content: Ok, out of kindness here is some turtle care needs and warnings for keeping turtles, I looked though the comments and noticed some people had outdated or incorrect turtle care recommendations and stuff. I just need people to understand this before getting turtles because I had read every comment and I do not want anyone getting turtles and unknowingly putting them at risk. And the one thing that bothered me the most in the comments is “you should get more turtles” or “Get him a friend “ like idk if you guys know but NO! 👉TURTLES👏 ARE 👏NOT 👏SOCIAL👏 ANIMALS 👏AND 👏THEY 👏WILL 👏HAVE 👏A 👏HIGH 👏POSSIBILITY 👏FOR👏 HARMING👏 IF 👏NOT 👏KILLING 👏EACH 👏OTHER! 👏IF 👏YOU 👏WANT 👏MORE👏 THEN 👏ONE 👏TURLE 👏IN👏THE👏SAME👏TANK👏THEN 👏YOU 👏WOULD 👏NEED 👏A 👏LARGER 👏TANK! 👏AND 👏EVEN 👏THEN 👏THERE 👏WOULD👏 ALWAYS 👏HAVE 👏A 👏RISK 👏OF 👏THEM👏BEING 👏HURT 👏OR 👏DYING! 👈 I’M NOT SAYING TO BE RUDE, I AM SAYING THAT TO PREVENT ANY IMPULSE BUYING THAT WOULD LEAD TO A HURT OR DEAD TURTLE AND PREVENTING A NEW OWNER FROM LEARNING THE HARD WAY THAT CAN BE HEARTBREAKING! 💔 So if you get a turtle the general rule for aquatic turtles is 10 gallons of water per inch of shell length, meaning a 4-inch turtle needs a minimum 40-gallon tank, and adults often require 75-120+ gallons; PUR TURTLE, DOUBLE THAT AMOUNT FOR TWO THOUGH I WOULD NOT RECOMMEND HOMEING THEM TOGETHER! this provides enough swimming space and accounts for their growth, but remember it's a minimum, and larger is always better. Always have a basking area that a turtle can get completely dry on and with that they need heat and UVB, preferably not the conjoined double lamp heat and UVB because the UVB light  in those kits aren’t the best, instead a tube UVB light that’s a 5T light is more recommend and make sure both the heat and UVB light hit the same basking area, also a water filter is also important and because turtles are messy, I say for example if you have one baby turtle in a 20 gallon long tank get a filter that is at least met for a 40-55 gallons (that what works for me at least with the tiny baby turtle) and as your turtle grows they will need to be upgraded so if you wanna save on money look into buying second hand tanks or using storage tubs or stock tanks, you don’t even have to fill the tank up all the way if it’s still a baby, you can start with a 75 gallon tank for example and keep it shallow and just raise the water level as it grows (keep in mind the water should be deep enough for it to swim but shallow enough to easily reach the surface to breathe, typically 1.5 to 2 times its shell length, or about 4-8 inches, gradually increasing as it grows stronger, ensuring it can always stand on its hind legs and touch the bottom or easily get to the surface.) and as for plants it depends on the turtle, I have some turtles that would ignore the plants (like my Mississippi map turtle) who would eat the romaine lettuce and sliced carrots but won’t eat the guppy grass in his tank or the hornwort. Or my baby red belly cooter who would eat every plant I give her besides the hornwort. Turns out turtles in the wild don’t care for hornwort do to the texture of the plant even if it’s safe to eat. None of my turtle eat or even like hornwort so you can try hornwort if you like just keep in mind it’s a floating plant,doesn’t need substrate. Also when it comes to substrate there is many options to choose from, you can do sand, tile, no substrate, large river rocks (make sure they are too big for the turtle to eat) but I would stay away from aquarium gravel because it can cause  impaction if eaten and there is many bad stores of turtles dying from aquarium gravel. As for decoration make sure that it’s something that would NOT crush the turtles, something that wouldn’t risk the turtle getting stuck, something that does not have blade like edges,and something that is aquarium safe that the turtle won’t eat, sometimes people use plastic plants but for me personally I wouldn’t trust it because I heard some turtles nip at it thinking it’s a real plant at first and we wouldn’t want our beloved turtles to eat plastic. Another thing is food, do NOT feed turtles  goldfish, rosy red minnows(any any minnows),and other cyprinids (like common minnows, dace) away from turtles because they contain thiaminase, an enzyme that destroys Vitamin B1, causing neurological damage, paralysis, and death in turtles, and also avoid aggressive fish like Cichlids that can harm the turtle's eyes. Slow, colorful, or long-finned fish like Bettas, Koi, or large Plecos are also bad choices as they are easy prey or attract unwanted attention, while fast, small livebearers (guppies, mollies, platies, and mosquito fish) are better options for enrichment or occasional treats, but always monitor for safety. Also turtles need a water heater, even more so in their baby and juvenile year! As for main food pellets there is many options just try and keep it diverse and try to avoid as much of the low quality ones if possible, for me personally I use Ultra Fresh Floating Turtle food, zoo med turtle food, and mazuri turtle food. Also almost forgot but make sure you had a cuddle bone for calcium,turtles need it for their shell health. ANYWAY Please make sure to do your research before getting a turtle let alone any pet, turtles are a big commitment and can live for many years! And if one day you end up not being able to care for the turtle do NOT release it into the wild, turtles brought up in captivity can not survive and if they do they can become invasive in some areas, best to responsibility find the turtle a new home if one day you decide you don’t want it or can’t care for it anymore!

text_content:
I am 70 yrs old and live in a mobile home park with a very large pond (football field) in the middle, well it went toxic last November ck, filled with foam and bacteria, years of poop and such, we also have a lot of ducks. Park tried to deal with, but out of their league and too cheap to hire pros, long story short is they all started dying, we had probably 100 sliders, up to 20-30 yrs old.  It was killing me watching them, die such slow deaths.  

I finally started rescuing the ones that were visibly were sick, gave them ABX shots etc. with the help of an online vet from ND (I am CA) saved 30 out of 34 and rehomed privately., Fast forward 4-5 months, came the babies.  The first round cost a lot of money and was hard on me for the 6 weeks nursing them back to health. Well, all my senior friends who walk around the pond with their dogs, first brought me 1 baby, then 2-3-4.  I kept saying please no more, I do not have the $$, capacity or strength to raise them to 4" (my heart is too sensitive) when I can adopt them out to a reptile store... BUT I can't turn them down either, they were the size of a quarter and pond still toxic.  

1 died after 4 months, I have no idea why I tested water almost daily food, basking etc. all checked. His name was Only (at the time the "only" one who made it) he was doing great and then just died on my 70th birthday, I am very sensitive to helpless creatures, and it wrecked me.  That's when the others started coming and now have 3.  

One look like she's (guessing) on steroids she's growing so fast and the other isn't growing as fast, probably male, they are in a 30-gallon tank together even though they say not to, they are best buds, the smaller rides on the bigger ones back.  Then came, 3 more recently and I had a 10 gallon that I already had bought for "Only".  I know I need bigger tanks, but I cannot afford nor have the room to go any bigger.  

Too small to adopt out in CA as it's illegal. The all of a sudden, the bigger one (Chance) was very lethargic, eyes looked swollen, (possibly upper respiratory), so I freaked out and took them both to animal ER which was a joke and "very" expensive. Then they seemed fine again, now Chance is doing it again, he won't bask, stays hanging on the water lettuce plant all day, BUT she is eating and swimming. I am at my wits end and emotionally it's killing me because I don't have the vast knowledge I need. I have a lot but they're still too small to give ABX shots safely. Every single item I read on internet, I am doing. 

If anyone can give me some advice it would be greatly appreciated.  This is a full-time job, and I am not that strong to lift tanks to completely clean, I have to get someone over to do that part. Water changes I am fine with and again water temps, food, cleanliness, etc. all CHECKED❤❤!  PLEASE HELP ME, I don't want to lose Chance, Sassy and Little one (name for now).😢😢 Thank you!

text_content: I have an around 19 year old res named kachu(punjabi word for turtle) that was gifted to me on my 3rd birthday. ashamed to say i didnt really care for her as children do and my parents basically treated her like a dog when she outgrew her tank. She spent a lot of time on the concrete floor of our backyard and fed regular fish pellets in water in a shallow basin that's kind of built into the floor, barely enough to submerge her fully. I'm turning 18 in a few days now and somehow gained consciousness ten years too late so ive pushed for her to be fed veggies and meats(boiled in water with no salt) along with some mildly expensive turtle pellets that she seems to like. she's nesting in my grandpa's garden now, even laid a single egg(i think she'll lay more, last time she laid around eight) and we're having a 1 meter x 2.5 meter x 0.5 meter pond built for her in the backyard, along with a dirt and grass patch to see if she stops eating all the plants my grandpa painstakingly planted. This video is a great help, and I sincerely hope to God that she forgives me for treating her so badly for half my life 😢

text_content: I appreciate that you pointed out that there are plenty of these turtles already in need of homes. One thing I wish you had added was the reason why these turtles are actually illegal in some places. Here in the Pacific Northwest, red eared-sliders are invasive, competing against our native painted and western pond turtles. People will either intentionally let their pets loose, or they will put their pets in a pond or stream in their yard, and it's not too hard for them to find their way to other bodies of water. Like you said, they are really hardy reptiles, which unfortunately makes them really good at establishing invasive populations, so it is important for people to be aware of just how hardy (and potentially harmful) they can be.

video_title: Rescued Turtle Update 🐢 #pets #animals #aquarium #wildlife #nature
text_content:
My turtle has grown a lot since the day he came to me 5 months ago. I have named him SIKKA because he was coin sized when I first saw him and also because he was strong enough to survive the conditions which dozens of turtles couldn't, at a wholesaler's place.

Below are certain tips that I have written down from my own experience (not from the internet):

• Big enough setup (not too deep if the turtle is small) Do not install high flow filter. Do not use small tubs and readymade plastic enclosures that are too small and fancy.
• Dry area (where it can climb and completely dry off) with turtle lamp above it to provide heat. It should he on for atleast 8 hours everyday. Turtles spends a lot of time in that area. You can search "Turtle Lamp" and "Turtle Platform" on any online shopping platform.
• Direct morning sunlight everyday for 1-2 hours in a small container, put 1-2 cup of water in it so that the turtle doesn't dehydrate.
• Install aquarium heater in the setup during winters. Set the heater at 24 degrees.
• Add small pieces of Turtle Calcium Block everytime you change water. 
• Give Saki Hikari Turtle food everyday, worms and insects sometimes as well. You should also feed boiled spinach, grated carrot or coriander 2-4 times a week. You can also feed a small piece of apple or banana once a week. When they are small, they need high protein to grow properly and as they age, they need more vegetable matter. Do not feed the turtle anything once every week, fasting is important too.
• Clean the tank once or twice a week. Clean water is very important to avoid shell rot, eye infections and respiratory problems.
