text_content: keeping water turtles is really really a lot of work. Keeping their water clean. I had 3 water turtles. i got them from ponds as hatchlings. one RES and 2 Eaatern Painted turtles. The one painted turtle was so small it would fit down the neck of a soda bottle. Probably would not have survived in the wild. they were ok till they got around 2 -3 inches. Even with a zoo med filter i still had to syphon off crud and about 1/3 of the water in a 20 gallon every other day. maybe i was feeding them too much. You also need a UV-B lamp so the turtles can metabolize calcium. Forget about sunlight coming through a window. Most window glass has UV blocker. If you want your turtles to see in color you will need a UV-A lamp as well.  you can give them all the calcium you can w/o UV it's wasted. Get a mealworm colony going in some oatmeal, feed a whole fish, internals and all. NO HAMBURGER, too rich for them. also try lettuce and greens. IMO RES are mean turtles. Painted turtles are friendly turtles. one pond i went to I saw about 10 painted turtles playing follow the leader, all the dips and dives, turns the other painted turtles did following. A quarry i swim in there was a painted turtle playing with young boys. the turtle would appear at the surface a few feet away and the boys laughing would swim towards the turtle, at the last moment the turtle would disappear only to pop up in back of them they would turn laughing and the process started all over again.

Kenan I've used dog kennel trays for years with my tortoises. Its shallow enough for small torts to get into but deep enough to stop any spillage. I also use dough proofing trays for water dishes on my larger torts. I bury them so  the tray is flush with the ground. That way it can't be flipped over if the tort steps on the side of the tray. text_content: I recently rescued a russian tortoise. Though not "abused", I'm afraid he was quite neglected. He was being kept in a large, plastic tub, under the dining room table without UVA/UVB lights, and without any special source of heat, other than what ever temp the mobile home was kept at, in Colorado. Not sure if he's ever really been outside, but I know his substrate was wood chips from their front yard (not good). I'm guessing his diet hasn't been the best either, but at least his waste was removed often. I believe they loved him, but didn't have any idea how to care for him. He is a trooper, though. Despite all this, he appears to be ok. I was in no way prepared to take him in, but did so gladly. I'd love if you could do an episode about shell rot and recognizing deficiencies in tortoises, and how to help them. My little guy has serious issues with his shell and I don't know what to do. Thanks!

I'm Brazilian and if this turtle genuinely came from the Amazon forest and was not raised in captivity it's possible that it was illegally trafficked. In Brazil, the sale and possession of Mata-Mata turtles are illegal according to the Environmental Crimes Law (Law No. 9,605/1998), and the Brazilian Institute of Environment and Renewable Natural Resources (IBAMA) also regulates the possession and trade of native species. I highly recommend you guys to research where this fish store sourced this turtle!!!

The sale and trade of Mata-Mata turtles for abroad can be legal in some cases, as long as all applicable laws and regulations are followed, both in the country of origin and the destination country. To legally export a Mata-Mata turtle as a pet, the exporter must obtain a CITES export permit from the country of origin and ensure that all local laws related to the possession and trade of wildlife are respected. In addition, the importer must ensure that the trade and possession of the turtle are legal in the destination country and, if necessary, obtain a CITES import permit or other relevant authorizations. You should ask the pet store for documentation regarding the origin of the Mata-Mata turtle and any required permits, such as CITES import permits or any other relevant authorizations.

Most <1m pythons are great for beginners because they don’t see you as food. Pythons usually use a wrap and strangle method to kill prey, and that means if they don’t see you as viable food, they won’t try to hurt you for the most part. 

Just remember, snakes have their own temperament, just like any other animal. If your snake prefers not to be handled very much or something like that, don’t torture it. I know snake owners who don’t ever interact with their snake other than to feed and change water because the snake gets stressed out. Pythons are known as big one brain cell sweeties, but not all of them are comfortable with always being around you or out of the enclosure much.

text_content:
I absolutely love berms, the ones I've known who have been handled were all sweethearts, and I've helped people get pretty decent sized ones (15ish foot was the largest berm) get used to being held. Even if they start off highly defensive (more helpful to keep in mind when a snake strikes it's a defensive act rather than aggressive one in most cases unless they honestly think your body part is food), I've found with daily work and proper care they tend to chill out relatively quickly.

The hardest one I helped a friend work with was her slightly larger reticulated python her son never touched then abandoned with her. She couldn't handle him but wanted to do her best to keep him 'cause she felt it wasn't his fault. His enclosure wasn't the best size, but once he calmed down he was usually chilling outside it.

I may love the actual giant snakes, but after a certain point size becomes the limiter, but it isn't just for size and danger level the way many think, after a certain size their feed animals get tricky. I mean- you can go to meat markets and farms to get prekilled animals telling them not to butcher the meat for you, but it gets tricky depending on where you live... even when they don't eat nearly as often as smaller snakes.

I wouldn't want to get an animal who need to eat feed animals larger than a rat, ideally frozen/thawed- and I feel bad about that 'cause I love rats as pets... unless it was fish. Far easier to go to a fish market to get whole fish of varying sizes if you're anywhere near port cities. SO easy.

So yeah- I'd sooner have an adult alligator where they can eat a wider range of meats and I wouldn't have to feed whole mammals and whole chickens consistently- though as any other reptile they need a varied diet, than a reticulated python- but to be fair, I wouldn't have alligators either.


Though I have known someone with a young alligator and "he" was awesome- so the temptation when I was told one could be gotten for me if I wanted was greater than when I was offered a breeding pair of anacondas for free.

Though won't lie- was actually tempted at the idea of them, my brain went to turning my basement apartment into a giant snake room with the sizable bedroom turned into a pool with a waterfall for them... then I thought "Nah- I really shouldn't own anything that could kill me..." and turned them down.


Edited to add- yeah- that part with you telling people what to do is needed if you aren't with people who know- when I helped with larger snakes where it goes into "I may not die but my ribs are breaking if not other bones..." mode and up I just have an open bottle of Listerine or a strong drinking alcohol around in case something happened and I get incapacitated and seriously have seconds to get it off- I think what people forget or don't think of is every time you breathe out they constrict more, so when you try to breathe in again you can't and so on until... ugh- I hate the idea of hurting a snake, but alcohol is one of the "easier" ways with a high likelyhood of success of getting a snake disengaged- that and grabbing the tail and pulling back can make continued constricting much harder for them, if not impossible- depending on the size.

Very very intentionally I've topped out my handling of the large snakes at 15 feet, I want 2 people with me at a min, esp if I know they're quirky- the alcohol of the non-rubbing kind uncapped and ready for someone to grab, and a phone close enough so 911 can be called. Only a few times I only had 1 other person, and a few times whoever was with me wasn't experienced. I have no interested with working with larger on any kind of regular basis...

text_content: My only concern with recommending anyone get a burmese python is the amount of space required for their enclosure. Yeah, you're right that the actual care isn't too hard, but I don't think most people understand or are able to supply the vast amount of space needed for them to live comfortably and thrive. We're talking like 4 ft by 8 ft floor space at the least for an adult. They can be such wonderful and active snakes when given the room they need, but often I see them housed in small tubs or small cages where they can really only have enough room to coil up and sit in a corner all day. I just hope you can provide the space needed and I'm hoping your new place provides that space. :)

video_title: Savannah Monitor, The Best Pet Lizard?
video_description: The savannah monitor (Varanus exanthematicus) is one of the coolest animals on planet Earth, but does that make it a good pet?
text_content:
I feed my monitor calci worms, horn worms, gut loaded dubias, calcium loaded eggs, few daily gut loaded crickets dusted in calcium, butterworms and wax worms only for his target training and mealworms for snacks. 
  He was an import wild caught young young baby but he was the calmest and FATTEST so I took him in and daily monitor every little thing about him. 
  I own a reptile rescue with 75 permanent resident forever family members "animals" and room for another 160 reacues rehabbers and rehomes.  My savannah monitor is in my top 3 favorite personal animals ever. He is my boy and my doll. Super sweet to handle.  I made sure to keep him warm but not around the action the first 60 days. With multiple small micro insect meals every day by me personally and tiny cameras watching him 24/7 so I could be sure he gets the best start for being a hatchling stressed out. He is now a fancy boy who i adore. Worth every bit of effort. I owe my years of experience especially with high needs rescued animals for the ease of owning my monitor.   I love tegus and other monitors but my savannah is my favorite by far of any lizzard ever.

video_title: Unboxing one of these rarest lizards I’ve ever seen! Axanthic savannah monitor!
text_content: I had to rescue a baby Savannah Monitor about 15yrs ago when I walked into an apartment and seen a large turtle in a tank so small it could not turn around which I took to my local petstore to save. Above that on the top shelf was a large 3x2ft terrarium but the entire front glass was literally smashed in with the broken glass still. I asked the guy's there what was in there? They laughed and one said there SHOULD be a lizard in there man lol I'm like no possible way? There was nothing but broken glass and a no working dry large waterfall feature...I then looked around that and to my shock there was a tiny 6 inch long Sav Monitor freezing cold to the touch and had not ate in who know's how long. I got very upset and told the guy's there I'm taking the animals and they said they are another guy's they just let him keep them there but he hadn't come back to even feed them. I made them come with me to the pet store to surrender the turtle after they tried to get me to just let it go in the local swamp...And I did not even know what a Sav Monitor was but I was determined to keep him was my first reptile ever...I kept him warm until I got him setup and started feeding him meat...within literal month's he was over 2ft long!! Absolutely amazing and powerful animals!! 👍

video_title: BABY Southern Alligator Lizard Care Guide | 2021
video_description: A comprehensive care guide on the Southern Alligator Lizard! Enjoy! - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - - #alligatorlizard #careguide ...
text_content: Pretty good care info, but they do like humidity as they usually live near water.  Southern Alligator Lizards ( Elgaria )  are semi arboreal and like to climb. My baby is in a tall viv and is always up high basking and hunting. You did not mention UVB and they DO need it for bone health and health. They need a low UVB 5% or the new Arcadia arboreal shadedweller kit.  There are breeders and taking an adult from the wild is not such a great thing. They are aggressive and stress out and are probably carrying parasites and when stressed can make them sick. Always take a fecal sample to your reptile vet to make sure.  Babies are delicate but hardy and will take to being handled much better. Keep temps on the lower side even for the basking area. They can dehydrate easy especially babies.  I had one for 15 years and this is my second one. I also have its Mexican cousin ,  A Mexican Alligator lizard ( Abronia Graminae)  Bright green with yellow eyes and have had her for over a year.

My least favourite social media comment is that I deprive Jack of water so she gulps her warm, parrot-safe tea. And I’m not mad about the people who think this - no one should be angry at them. Animals ARE abused and neglected for social media, and we shouldn’t silence concern - not genuine concern that comes from a place of care for animals. When you silence it, it has this slow and quiet effect and that’s bad.  It helps people get away with stuff that they should be held to account for. But speak up with kindness and concern, rather than assumption and anger. That’s when people are more inclined to listen.

P.S please know that my life is not worth living if I do not boil the kettle, give Jack a spoon to throw AND MAKE THAT BIRD HER TEA. Jack makes most of the rules here and please don’t write any comments asking if Jack is abusing her human because Jack prefers silence over airing those concerns (Jack is the exception to most rules). 

P.s Jack’s vits are mixed into half a mug of cold water, and kettle water then added - stirred and cooled if needed, or if it’s not warm enough for her, more hot added. I always check the temperature before giving her the cup!

video_description: I Adopted a Cockatoo from a Rescue — Now He Sings to Me Every Day ❤️ ❤️ This is the story of a Cockatoo who refused to ...
text_content: I’ve lived nearly 40 years of my life with up to 4 parrots. I discovered a CH conure in a feed store. He was untamed and open banded. It was 1988 and quarantine stations were still legal in California at the time. He had been trying to take baths in the back of the fish tanks. The lady who owned the feed store was a friend and she wanted a good home for him so she gave me a cage and all the supplies for $35 and gave me the bird. She called it a promotion. She just fell in love with him and even though she didn’t want to keep him she knew I would give him the best home. It was a challenge as I had never hand tamed a wild parrot that had never been handled. Luckily there was a robust parrot companion community and I learned how to tame him. My spouse was active duty military at the time and so he was out on a ship at sea in the West Pacific, I had nothing to do in my spare time when I wasn’t at work. My new conure had a pretty portable standard pacific parrot cage. I took him with me whenever I went house sitting or visiting my uncles place in Huntington Beach. So my parrots have always been members of the family and not just exotic pet wallpaper to impress people with. A decades long relationship with a parrot has its ups and downs but as the years go by, the closeness is really priceless. I find the more time we spend together the closer our bond gets. Are there times you get frustrated with them? Of course but I think if you understand how your kind of bird thinks and behavior can be put into that context, you can start to really unlock the key to providing a rewarding life for both you and your birds. I’ve a lot of experience with parrots but I still don’t feel qualified to adopt a cockatoo. They need to be the center of attention and from what I’ve seen especially with Moluccan’s they have a lot of energy. They are so special though just absolutely adorable.

video_title: Parrot Who Can't Walk Builds a New Life One Step at a Time
video_description: This story follows Gina, a parrot whose feet were badly broken by a human and healed the wrong way. Unable to perch or walk ...
text_content:
It really angers me knowing Gina's toes and foot bones were broken, and then _she was left to suffer that way._ 

We always hope that only decent, caring humans keep other species as companions, but then we are confronted with the ugly truth. 

Divine intervention occurred when Michele found out about Gina and did everything in her power to bring Gina home with her. 

And Gina knew this, too. Birds can perceive differences between humans, and Gina realized that trustworthy and loving humans _do_ exist. 

It's why she is recovering so well, considering the extent of  permanent disability and disfigurement she was left with. Which was on purpose, it appears.

Having adopted a smaller parrot who went through the same kind of physical abuse and who receiving no treatment and care, I also sought veterinary help for him after freeing him from his captors (Little Star). 

A twisted, hobbling little bird who was the size of a 3-month old instead of an adult, the veterinarian who examined him was holding back tears on realizing someone did to Little Star what had been done to Gina. There was no way to repair the damage once the bones had knit. 

He devised a rehabilitation plan for me to work with Little Star, who also "walked on his legs" instead of his feet. 

These precious birds don't deserve to suffer, let alone purposefully. 

In the end, for however long Gina's life will be, at least she will be safe, loved, given the medical and flock care she needs and deserves, all thanks to a _good_ lady with a humble soul, a big heart, and common sense.

_Thank goodness, thank goodness, thank goodness._

text_content:
Lovely wee guy! Thank you for working so well on him. He looks much happier now!!

I'm always amazed at captured turtles that do well in captivity from thw wild. About 25ish years ago, an ex and I came upon 2 baby red-eared sliders in 2 different spots about 6 weeks apart when fishing. One had just lost an eye- it was a raw mess, and one just lost a leg. We got them home and I had an old tank that was empty so to the petstore we went for supplies, got them seen at a vet, and got their wounds cleaned. They were healing and doing ok, eating and drinking and pooping. However around 2 weeks in, both began flipping out. The one missing an eye would smack himself into the same corner repeatedly. The other one began something similar but not as violent. I have no idea why, the vet also had no idea why but just said that although we gave them a large tank with water and good substrate, that they still were likely unhappy being confined and recommended us to let them go. We brought them both to a quieter pond that didn't have large fish or snapping turtles and let them go. I always wondered 1. if they survived to adulthood with one minus an eye and one minus a limb and 2. If so, would they find each other and live together somehow. I never set out to bring home baby turtles, I couldn't just leave them like that. I hope they made it.
